title: Making a leather document holder
subtitle: Saddle-stitch a flat vegetable-tanned leather folio with two internal pockets for A4 documents and a card slot
excerpt: How to make a flat leather document holder from veg-tan leather. Covers cutting the main body and internal pocket panels, skiving the fold edges, stitching the pockets onto the body, and finishing the spine fold with a burnished edge.
sourceNotes: Traditional leatherwork bookbinding and flat-case technique. Al Stohlman, The Art of Hand Sewing Leather (referenced principles, not reproduced). UK leatherworking trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat, two-panel leather case designed to carry documents flat. The two panels are the full width of the finished folio and are joined at the spine by a stitched or folded hinge. The interior carries pockets (simple slash-bottom pockets formed by stitching a smaller leather panel to the inner face of each body panel. A leather folio is a more durable alternative to a fabric conference folder; it ages with use and acquires a patina over years of handling.

content.text: holds A4 documents flat in two internal pockets and folds in half at the spine. The approach is cutting the outer body in one piece, scoring the spine fold line, cutting and stitching the pockets, and finishing all edges by burnishing. A 1.5 mm veg-tan gives a folio that is firm enough to hold its shape but flexible at the spine.

Cut the main body panel from 1.5 mm veg-tan: 640 mm × 235 mm) two A4 panels side by side (each 320 mm × 235 mm) with the spine line at the centre. Mark the spine fold line at 320 mm with a bone folder score pressed firmly along a steel rule. Bevel all four edges with the edge beveller.

Cut two pocket panels: each 300 mm × 190 mm. The pocket top edge (the open edge) should be skived and burnished for a clean finish. Skive the three stitching edges on the flesh side to reduce thickness at the stitch line. Mark the stitch line 8 mm from each stitching edge.

Position each pocket on the corresponding body panel, centred with 15 mm margins from body edges. Punch stitch holes through both pocket and body together using a pricking iron at 3 mm spacing along three sides (bottom and both sides, not the open top. Saddle-stitch the three sides with waxed linen thread. A card-width slot can be added to one pocket by stitching a 100 × 70 mm piece to the pocket face before the pocket is stitched to the body.

Fold the body at the scored spine line. The body will fold cleanly at the score. Burnish all exterior body edges with water and the burnisher. Burnish the spine fold line on the outside) the compression hardens the leather at the hinge and prevents it cracking with use. A thin line of beeswax burnished into the spine fold further conditions the hinge.
